worth_it_explanation: Integral Ad Science (IAS) Signal is where measurement configurations, suitability profiles, and brand-safety settings live. A user who can alter those settings can change what IAS flags as unsafe, how viewability is counted, or how fraud thresholds are applied, all of which flow directly into campaign decisions and reporting. These checks are fast to complete inside IAS Signal account settings, the fixes are an access-level adjustment or a user removal, and the result is an access model that reflects who actually needs to configure IAS versus who only needs to read its reports.
intro: IAS Signal uses a role-based access model that separates users by what they can do in the platform. Users with configuration-capable access can modify measurement settings, suitability profiles, and verification parameters. Users with reporting-only access can view dashboards and pull data without changing how measurement is applied. The distinction matters because a change to a suitability profile or a fraud threshold affects every campaign that profile is attached to. These checks confirm that the access levels assigned in IAS Signal match each user's actual responsibilities, that no accounts belonging to departed staff or former agency contacts remain active, and that reporting-side users cannot alter the settings that govern how IAS measures and reports.

audit_checks.title: Users hold configuration-capable access when their work is limited to reporting
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A user with configuration-capable access in IAS Signal can modify measurement profiles, suitability categories, and verification parameters. Assigning that level of access to analysts or agency contacts whose work is limited to reading dashboards and pulling reports creates an unnecessary surface for accidental or unauthorized changes to settings that govern how verification is applied across active campaigns.

educate.base:
IAS Signal separates users by what they are permitted to do in the platform. Users with configuration-capable access can create and modify measurement profiles, adjust suitability categories, set fraud avoidance thresholds, and change how verification is applied to campaigns or line items. Users with reporting-only access can view dashboards, pull data, and export reports without touching any of those settings.

When configuration-capable access is assigned broadly rather than only to users whose jobs require it, any of those users can alter a setting that affects campaigns they are not responsible for. A suitability profile change that unintentionally widens the category exclusions, or a threshold adjustment that changes how a fraud signal is weighted, takes effect across every campaign the profile is attached to. The person who made the change may not recognize the downstream impact because their day-to-day work is in reporting, not configuration.

The appropriate model is to assign configuration-capable access only to users who are responsible for setting up and maintaining IAS measurement profiles, and to assign reporting-only access to analysts, client-side contacts, and anyone else whose job is to read the data rather than change how it is produced.

A few patterns suggest access may be broader than necessary:
  • Analysts who build performance decks from IAS Signal data hold access that also lets them modify the suitability or fraud settings behind that data
  • Agency reporting contacts hold configuration-capable access because it was faster to provision than a more restricted level
  • Multiple users hold the highest available access level even though only one or two are responsible for measurement configuration
You may want to review the user list in IAS Signal account settings and compare each person's access level against their actual responsibilities before making any adjustments.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser, the analyst reviewing IAS viewability data alongside return on ad spend (ROAS) typically needs reporting access only. You may want to confirm that ecommerce-side contacts who pull viewability or fraud data for weekly pacing reviews do not also hold access that lets them modify the measurement profiles those reports draw from.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, a client-side contact reviewing IAS brand-safety data for compliance purposes typically needs reporting access. You may want to confirm that contacts reviewing suitability reports do not hold access that lets them alter the suitability categories the reports are built on.

educate.learn_more_note: IAS Signal user-management documentation is accessible to credentialed account holders through the IAS Signal platform settings. Contact your Integral Ad Science account representative for role configuration guidance specific to your account.

investigate.intro: Review the user list in IAS Signal account settings and compare each user's access level against their day-to-day responsibilities in the platform.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal and navigate to the account or organization settings. Locate the user management section, which lists each user alongside their assigned access level.
For each user with configuration-capable access (the level that allows modifying measurement profiles, suitability settings, or fraud thresholds), note their name, email address, and access level.

For each configuration-capable user, confirm whether their actual work involves creating or modifying IAS measurement settings. A user whose job is pulling reports or reviewing dashboards is a candidate for a more restricted, reporting-only access level.

Note any configuration-capable users who are agency contacts or third-party vendors rather than members of the team directly responsible for IAS setup. Agency reporting contacts typically need reporting access, not configuration access.
Document each mismatch: user name, current access level, actual job function, and the access level their responsibilities require.

evaluate.logic: For each user flagged during the review, record their name, current access level, and whether that level matches their actual responsibilities. A user whose access level matches their job function passes. A user whose access level grants configuration rights they do not use or need is a fail, recorded with the user's name, current access level, and the access level their work requires.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each user's name, current access level, actual job responsibilities, and whether the access level is appropriate to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s IAS Signal account includes users holding configuration-capable access whose day-to-day work is limited to reviewing reports and dashboards. Configuration-capable access in IAS Signal allows modifying measurement profiles, suitability settings, and fraud thresholds, any of which can alter how verification is applied across active campaigns. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the user list against each person's actual responsibilities and moving reporting-only users to the appropriate reporting access level, reserving configuration-capable access for the team members who set up and maintain IAS measurement profiles.

audit_checks.title: Departed staff or former agency contacts still hold active IAS Signal access
audit_checks.impact_rationale: IAS Signal does not expire or remove user access automatically when an employment relationship or agency engagement ends. A former team member or agency contact who held configuration-capable access retains the ability to view measurement data, modify suitability profiles, and change verification settings for as long as their account remains active. The account stays usable after the relationship ends without the current team being aware.

educate.base:
IAS Signal user access is managed manually. There is no automatic deprovisioning tied to an employment end date, a contract expiration, or a change in agency roster. A user account stays active at whatever access level it was last set to until an administrator explicitly removes it.

A former employee who held configuration-capable access in IAS Signal retains the ability to view campaign measurement data and, if the account is not removed, to modify verification settings governing active campaigns. A former agency contact who managed IAS profiles retains visibility into measurement data, suitability configurations, and reporting that may include sensitive campaign or performance information. Neither of these situations requires deliberate misuse to become a problem: a login from an unrevoked former employee credential, or a compromised account at a former agency, can produce changes that are difficult to trace after the fact.

Stale access arises in predictable ways. An offboarding process that covers corporate email, virtual private network (VPN), and buying-platform access but omits IAS Signal leaves the access open. An agency transition that removes a contact from internal systems does not propagate to third-party platforms unless someone explicitly completes that step. A vendor relationship that ended months ago may still appear as an active user in the IAS Signal account.

A few patterns point to stale access in the user list:
  • User accounts associated with email addresses at agencies or vendors the client no longer works with
  • User accounts for email addresses that are no longer active in the client's corporate directory or identity provider
  • Users who have not logged in for an extended period but still hold configuration-capable access
You may want to cross-reference the IAS Signal user list against the client's current roster of active employees and active agency or vendor relationships before removing anyone. A user who appears inactive may still hold a legitimate role.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser, a former agency contact who ran past display or video campaigns may still hold access to IAS measurement data that includes performance and conversion-context information. You may want to confirm that access for past agency teams was removed when the relationship ended and not only when the campaign went dark.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, a former contact with configuration-capable access can still view IAS suitability and fraud data associated with lead-generation campaigns. You may want to confirm that contacts from ended engagements were removed from IAS Signal and not only from the media buying platform.

educate.learn_more_note: IAS Signal user-management documentation is accessible to credentialed account holders through the IAS Signal platform settings. Contact your Integral Ad Science account representative for guidance on user deprovisioning and offboarding procedures.

investigate.intro: Pull the full user list in IAS Signal account settings and cross-reference it against the client's current roster of active employees, active agency contacts, and active vendor relationships.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal and navigate to the account or organization settings. Open the user management section and note the full list of active users, including each user's name, email address, and access level.
Review each email domain in the user list against the client's current agency roster and vendor relationships. Flag any email domain belonging to an agency or vendor the client no longer works with.
For internal users, cross-reference the email addresses against the client's current corporate directory or identity provider. Flag any address that is no longer active.
For each flagged user, confirm with the client's account owner whether the person still has a legitimate reason to hold IAS Signal access. Remove accounts the client confirms are no longer active.

Note whether IAS Signal shows a last-login date or activity timestamp for each user. Users who have not logged in for an extended period and whose access level is configuration-capable are higher-priority candidates for review.

evaluate.logic: For each flagged user, record their name, email domain, access level, and whether the client confirms they are still active. A user confirmed as currently active with a legitimate role passes. A user the client cannot confirm as active, or whose email domain belongs to a former agency or vendor, is a fail, recorded with the user's name, email, access level, and the reason for the flag.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each flagged user's name, email, access level, and active-or-departed status to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} user accounts in {{site_name}}'s IAS Signal platform that belong to contacts who are no longer part of the active account team or whose continued association with the account cannot be confirmed. These accounts retain the access level assigned when the relationship was active, which in some cases includes configuration-capable access to measurement profiles and suitability settings. {{voice_recommend}} cross-referencing the full IAS Signal user list against the client's current employee and agency roster, removing any account the client confirms is no longer active, and adding an IAS Signal access review to the standard offboarding checklist whenever a team member, agency contact, or vendor rotates off the account.

audit_checks.title: No separation between users who configure measurement settings and users who report on them
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When configuration-capable and reporting-only users are not separated in IAS Signal, anyone who can view measurement data can also change the parameters behind it. A person reviewing brand-safety or viewability results can, whether intentionally or not, alter a suitability profile, adjust a fraud threshold, or modify the measurement settings that produced the data they are reviewing. This collapses the control that would otherwise require two separate parties to both change and approve a configuration adjustment.

educate.base:
In a well-structured IAS Signal account, the users who configure measurement and suitability settings are not the same users who review and report on those settings' outputs. This separation serves a practical purpose: a change to an IAS configuration requires deliberate action by someone responsible for IAS setup, and reporting-side users cannot inadvertently alter the parameters behind data they are analyzing.

IAS Signal settings that can affect measurement outputs include measurement profile assignments (which determine what verification signals IAS applies to a campaign), suitability category selections (which define what content is flagged as brand-unsafe), fraud avoidance thresholds (which govern how aggressive IAS's fraud filtering is), and viewability measurement parameters. A change to any of these takes effect immediately for the campaigns the profile is attached to and affects the data that reporting users then see.

When no separation exists and all users hold configuration-capable access, several problems become harder to manage. Auditing who changed a setting and whether that person was authorized to do so becomes difficult. A reporting-side analyst who makes an exploratory configuration change alters live measurement for campaigns they may not be responsible for. A client-side contact reviewing performance data can change the IAS settings governing that data in the same session they use to pull a report.

A few signs suggest that configuration and reporting access are not separated:
  • All or most users in IAS Signal hold the same access level, with no distinction between those who set up measurement and those who use its reports
  • The IAS Signal account has no defined group of users explicitly limited to read or reporting access
  • Configuration changes to measurement profiles or suitability settings cannot be attributed to a specific user or role through any available account activity log
You may want to identify which users are responsible for IAS configuration decisions and which users consume IAS data in a reporting capacity, then confirm that the access levels in IAS Signal reflect that distinction.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser, the analyst who reviews IAS viewability data alongside ROAS (return on ad spend) to evaluate media quality is performing a reporting function. You may want to confirm that this analyst does not also hold access that lets them modify the viewability measurement profile the ROAS analysis draws from. A profile change mid-flight would alter the measurement baseline the analysis depends on.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, the team member reviewing IAS brand-safety flags to assess whether suitability settings are too restrictive is performing an analytical function. You may want to confirm that any resulting adjustment to the suitability settings is made by the person responsible for IAS configuration, rather than the analyst who surfaced the question.

educate.learn_more_note: IAS Signal user-management documentation is accessible to credentialed account holders through the IAS Signal platform settings. Contact your Integral Ad Science account representative for guidance on structuring access levels to separate configuration and reporting functions.

investigate.intro: Review the user list in IAS Signal account settings to determine whether users are differentiated by access level, and whether that differentiation maps to the distinction between configuration responsibilities and reporting responsibilities.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal and navigate to the account or organization settings. Open the user management section and note the access level assigned to each user.

Identify users who are responsible for IAS configuration decisions: setting up measurement profiles, assigning profiles to campaigns, adjusting suitability categories, and changing fraud thresholds. These users should hold configuration-capable access.
Identify users whose work is limited to reviewing IAS dashboards, pulling reports, or analyzing data for campaign decisions. These users should hold reporting-only access.
Compare the two groups against the actual access levels in IAS Signal. Flag any user in the reporting group who holds configuration-capable access.

If IAS Signal provides an activity or change log, review it for configuration changes made by users whose role is primarily reporting-oriented. Any such change is a signal that the separation is not functioning as intended.

evaluate.logic: For each user, record whether their access level matches their functional role: configuration or reporting. A user with configuration-capable access who is responsible for IAS setup passes. A user with configuration-capable access whose function is reporting and who is not responsible for IAS configuration is a fail, recorded with the user's name, current access level, and their actual function. An account where all users hold the same access level with no reporting-only tier is a fail at the account level.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each user's name, access level, and functional role in the account to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s IAS Signal account does not separate users by configuration access and reporting access: all or most users hold a level of access that lets them both review measurement data and modify the settings behind it. This means that anyone reviewing a brand-safety or viewability report can also change the measurement profile or suitability settings that produced the data. {{voice_recommend}} identifying which users are responsible for IAS configuration decisions and assigning reporting-only access to all others, so that changes to measurement profiles, suitability categories, and fraud thresholds require action by a designated configuration owner rather than being available to any user who logs in to pull a report.
